Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd have these bursts where I'd study for hours, watching videos and going through materials, but then I'd disappear for days. I'm taking six online courses, so I kind of just bounced between them based on what felt urgent or interesting at the moment. I never really had a set schedule - I'd study when I felt like it or when anxiety about deadlines kicked in.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, definitely driven by deadlines more than anything consistent. Some weeks I'd barely touch the course, then suddenly I'd cram 31 hours of studying into a few days when something was due. I know it's not the healthiest approach, but when you're juggling multiple courses and extracurriculars, you just deal with whatever's most pressing. The variability kept me on edge sometimes.
Q3: Usually it was either panic about an upcoming deadline or boredom scrolling through my phone and remembering I should probably check something. Sometimes I'd get a notification about a new assignment or announcement and that would prompt me to log in. But I won't lie - there were plenty of notifications I just swiped away and ignored. I logged in maybe 15 times total, which sounds bad when I say it out loud.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me - it always felt like this thing I had to force myself to visit. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more regularly, especially early in the semester when I had good intentions. But those routines always fell apart once life got busy or I found other ways to access the materials I needed.
Q5: The videos were definitely the most useful for me since I'm a visual learner. I watched almost 89 minutes of video content, and that's where most of my actual learning happened. I could pause, rewind, and really absorb the concepts at my own pace. Diagrams and visual explanations in the lecture recordings stuck with me way better than reading through text-heavy documents.
Q6: The discussion forums were basically dead to me - I think I posted maybe twice the entire course. They felt performative, like I was just writing to prove I existed rather than actually learning anything. I also skipped a lot of the supplementary readings and optional practice exercises. When you're stretched thin across six courses, you learn to cut corners on things that won't directly impact your grade.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to the exam or assignments. I'm pretty strategic about where I invest my energy - if something was going to help me score well, I was in. The videos felt worthwhile because I could see the concepts showing up on tests. Busy work or activities that seemed disconnected from assessment? Those got deprioritized fast.
Q8: Oh, completely different. Required activities got done - eventually, even if I was cutting it close on deadlines. Optional stuff was basically invisible to me unless I was genuinely curious or had extra time, which was rare. There's this mental calculation I do where optional means 'nice to have but won't hurt me to skip.' With my anxiety levels, I had to pick my battles carefully.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a ton of materials early on and studied from those files on my tablet without being connected to the LMS. I also used YouTube videos from other professors explaining the same concepts, sometimes in ways that clicked better for me. My notes app is full of stuff I typed up while studying that the LMS would never know about. External resources were honestly a lifesaver.
Q10: There were so many times I was deep in studying but the LMS would show nothing. I'd have the lecture slides printed out, spread across my desk, working through problems by hand. Or I'd be in a study group with peers - we'd meet up and work through concepts together for hours. My 48 peer interactions outside the system probably taught me more than anything I did logged in.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my whole approach. I'd log in once, download everything I needed for the week, then work offline. It was more convenient and I didn't have to deal with spotty internet or slow loading times. I'd watch a video once in the LMS, take screenshots of key slides, and then review those screenshots dozens of times without ever logging back in.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. My anxiety spiked pretty hard around then, and even though I felt motivated, I just couldn't bring myself to engage consistently. I was also dealing with extracurricular commitments that ate into my time. There were weeks where I'd open the LMS, feel overwhelmed by everything piling up, and just close it again. The CBT sessions I did helped me manage that better eventually.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed, the LMS became this thing I avoided because it represented all the work I wasn't doing. Competing responsibilities from my other five courses meant this one sometimes got neglected. Confusion about certain topics made me procrastinate on those specific modules - I'd tell myself I'd figure it out later and then later never came until right before the exam.
Q14: All the time. I'd log in, scroll through announcements, maybe click on a few things, but my brain was elsewhere. Sometimes I was so anxious about everything I needed to do that I couldn't actually focus on any single thing. I'd have videos playing while I was mentally planning my week or worrying about other deadlines. Present but not really there, you know?
Q15: The assignments I found confusing or tedious got pushed back constantly. I knew they were important - my assignment completion was only around 48% which is embarrassing - but the anxiety of not understanding something made me avoid it. It's this weird cycle where avoiding it made the anxiety worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like relief. I'm working on that.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd ignore the LMS for weeks, then suddenly become hyperactive when something was due. My login patterns probably looked like flat lines punctuated by spikes of panic-driven activity. It's not ideal, but deadlines gave me the external pressure I needed to actually sit down and do the work. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. My exam scores were actually really strong - over 91% - so that positive reinforcement kept me confident about my approach even when my attendance and completion rates were low. Negative or critical feedback sometimes made me avoid that area for a while, which I know isn't productive but it's an honest reaction.
Q18: The course structure was flexible enough that I could get away with my inconsistent approach, which was both good and bad. Good because it accommodated my chaotic schedule, bad because it didn't force me into better habits. The way materials were organized made it easy to identify what was essential versus supplementary, so I could strategically engage with just enough to succeed.
Q19: Having videos as a primary resource made things easier since that matches how I learn best. But the discussion requirements felt forced and didn't add much value for me. The sheer volume of content across my six courses made it hard to engage deeply with any single one. If the course had more visual summaries or concept maps, I probably would have engaged more consistently.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing anything - kind of like checking if the stove is off multiple times. Or maybe they're genuinely trying to understand the material but struggling with the actual assignments. High activity doesn't mean productive activity. Sometimes clicking around is a form of procrastination disguised as engagement.
Q21: That's literally me. My LMS activity was low, but I scored a distinction because I studied effectively outside the system. I knew what I needed to learn, found efficient ways to learn it, and performed when it counted. The LMS is just one tool - it doesn't capture studying from downloaded materials, peer discussions, or the mental processing that happens when you're not logged in.
Q22: Course logs can show when you logged in and what you clicked on, but they can't show comprehension or effort. They miss offline studying, peer collaboration, and the quality of engagement versus just the quantity. Someone could click through every video without absorbing anything, and the log would look great. Meanwhile, someone studying deeply from printed notes looks disengaged. It's a limited picture.
Q23: For me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just going through the motions. It's when I'm curious about a concept and want to dig deeper, or when I'm actively connecting ideas rather than passively consuming content. It's a mental state more than a measurable behavior. You can be deeply engaged while staring at the ceiling thinking through a problem.
Q24: The LMS would never show my anxiety levels or how much mental energy I spent just managing my stress about the course. It wouldn't capture the peer study sessions that were crucial to my understanding, or the hours I spent with downloaded materials. It also can't show that despite looking disengaged on paper, I was actually highly motivated and ultimately succeeded. Numbers don't tell the whole story.
